title: The Quantitative Social Scientist
text:
“Expertise is still the result of rigorous engagement with information.”
This is a reference you grow into, not a course you finish. It walks the road from basic probability to the academic rigour of causal inference and the messy realities of field and client work — always intuition first, examples second, and mathematics used in a common-sense way to explain principles and deviations, with the full derivations kept in an appendix you can reach for but never have to wade through.
It is built on a curated library of canonical sources (Wooldridge, Angrist & Pischke, Cunningham, Gerber & Green, Ross, ISLR, and more), and it is designed to pair with a NotebookLM study companion — see How to use this.

title: The Quantitative Social Scientist
text:
The road
One rising arc, from “what is chance?” to “what causes what?” — undergraduate to graduate, intuition first. The spine climbs to causal inference as its summit; machine learning runs as a separate, bridged track you are pointed to when a predictive question shows up.


The ascent

Overture — How Research Works orientationWhy we do any of this: questions, measurement, designs, ethics.
Foundations: Probability & Data basicThe grammar of uncertainty; describing real data.
Inference undergradSampling, the CLT, estimation, testing, Bayesian inference.
Statistical Modelling: Regression undergradOLS, the regression workhorse, diagnostics, limited outcomes.




To the summit

Predictive Modelling graduateThe computational turn: bias–variance, cross-validation, time series, the debates.
The Summit: Causal Inference graduatePotential outcomes, RCTs, the “Furious Five”, DAGs, synthetic control.
Doing Research in the World appliedMeasurement, communicating uncertainty, the tyranny of metrics.



Side tracks

ML Track — learning, regularisation, trees, heterogeneity. Bridged from the spine.
Appendix · Mathematics — the proofs, on demand.
Appendix · Computing — R-first, with Python alongside.

title: The Quantitative Social Scientist
text:
How to read an article
Every entry follows the same shape, so you always know where to stand:

In one sentence — the intuition kernel.
Why it matters / where it sits — the use case before the formula.
Intuition — the idea in plain language, with examples.
Worked examples — drawn from the source library.
The math, where it earns its place — minimal, inline, in service of the idea.
Common pitfalls — how this gets misused in practice.
From the sources — exactly which book says what.
Appendix — the full mathematics, for when you want the proof of thought.
Study with NotebookLM — prompts to interrogate the sources yourself.

Start with the flagship entry to see the style in full: Sampling distributions & the Central Limit Theorem →

title: VI · Doing Research in the World
text: Academic training tends to stop at the clean estimate. Real research — in the field, for a client, inside an organisation — begins there. This module is what separates someone who can run a model from someone who can be trusted with a question. It is the project’s distinctive layer, and the source library is unusually strong here.

title: VI · Doing Research in the World
text:
The critical conscience
Two sources give this module its edge. Muller’s The Tyranny of Metrics is the warning that measuring a thing changes behaviour around it — that the number you optimise can quietly become the goal, displacing what it was meant to track. Reinhart’s Statistics Done Wrong keeps the methods honest. Together they make the case that judgement — knowing when not to trust the number — is the final skill of the quantitative researcher.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
VI · Doing Research in the World

title: VI · Doing Research in the World
text:
Where this leads
This is the destination of the main track: a researcher who holds academic rigour and industry realities in the same hand.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
VI · Doing Research in the World

title: IV · The Computational Turn: Predictive Modelling
text:
This is the hinge of the manual: where statistical thinking (estimate an effect, defend it, quantify uncertainty) meets computational thinking (build something that predicts well on data it has never seen). It is the on-ramp to the causal summit — you meet cross-validation, regularization, and the bias–variance trade-off here, because modern causal inference uses these tools — and it gathers the field’s running debates in one place.
There are two distinct goals people pursue with the same regression output, and conflating them is the source of endless confusion in both academia and industry:
crumbs: IV · The Computational Turn: Predictive Modelling

The chapter stands on ISLR and the explanatory-modelling notes.

Two ways onward: deeper into the ML Track (the predictive culture’s full toolkit), and upward to the summit — Part V · Causal Inference, where these computational tools are bent to the hardest question of all: what causes what?

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text: Every statistic you compute is a random variable in disguise: run the study again and you’d get a slightly different number. The sampling distribution is the set of numbers you could have gotten, and the Central Limit Theorem tells you that — for averages — that set is almost always a bell curve.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Why it matters
You run a survey, compute an average, and report it. But you only ran the survey once. Had a different 1,200 people answered the phone, your average would have come out a little different. So the real question behind every empirical claim is: how much would my answer have bounced around if chance had dealt a different sample?
That bounce is not a nuisance to apologize for — it is the thing inference is about. Margins of error, confidence intervals, p-values, standard errors: every one of them is a statement about a sampling distribution. Get this idea and the rest of statistics stops being a bag of formulas and becomes one question asked many ways.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Intuition
There are two different distributions that beginners constantly confuse, and keeping them apart is most of the battle. (Bruce et al. 2020)

A data distribution describes the individual values in one dataset — the heights of the people in your sample, say. Plot a histogram of your data and that’s what you see.
A sampling distribution describes how a summary of the data — the mean, a proportion, a treatment effect — would vary across many hypothetical datasets drawn the same way.

The leap is this: a statistic is a function of random data, so it is itself random. The sample mean \bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum X_i is a sum of random variables, and a sum of random variables has its own probability distribution. (Ross 2018) Grinstead & Snell make this concrete by simulation: keep drawing samples, compute the mean each time, and watch those means scatter into a shape of their own. (Grinstead and Snell 1997) In an experiment, Gerber & Green give the cleanest gloss — the sampling distribution is “the collection of estimates that could have been generated by every possible random assignment.” That is exactly what lets you ask whether your result was a lucky draw. (Gerber and Green 2012)

Watch it happen
The most convincing argument here is not a proof — it’s a picture. Start with a population that is emphatically not bell-shaped (a heavily skewed one), draw samples, and plot the distribution of the sample mean as the sample size grows.


Show the simulation code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)

# A strongly right-skewed "population": an exponential distribution.
# (Think: income, donation sizes, time-to-event — common in social data.)
pop_mean = 1.0
population = rng.exponential(scale=pop_mean, size=200_000)

sample_sizes = [2, 10, 30]
n_reps = 50_000

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(11, 3.1))

# Panel 0: the raw population
axes[0].hist(population, bins=60, range=(0, 6), color="#9a6a2f", alpha=0.85)
axes[0].set_title("Population\n(raw data, skewed)", fontsize=10)
axes[0].set_yticks([])

# Panels 1-3: sampling distribution of the mean at increasing n
for ax, n in zip(axes[1:], sample_sizes):
    means = population[rng.integers(0, population.size, size=(n_reps, n))].mean(axis=1)
    ax.hist(means, bins=60, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.85, density=True)
    ax.set_title(f"Mean of n = {n}", fontsize=10)
    ax.set_yticks([])
    # overlay the Normal the CLT predicts: mean = pop_mean, sd = pop_sd / sqrt(n)
    pop_sd = population.std()
    xs = np.linspace(means.min(), means.max(), 200)
    se = pop_sd / np.sqrt(n)
    normal = np.exp(-0.5 * ((xs - pop_mean) / se) ** 2) / (se * np.sqrt(2 * np.pi))
    ax.plot(xs, normal, color="#b5446e", lw=1.8)

fig.suptitle("The sample mean forgets the shape it came from", fontsize=12)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A skewed population (left) versus the sampling distribution of its mean at n = 2, 10, and 30. The raw data is lopsided, yet the averages march toward a bell.





The leftmost panel is the world as it actually is: lopsided. Yet by the time we average just 30 observations, the distribution of that average is almost perfectly Normal (the pink curve is the bell the theorem predicts). The sample mean forgets the shape it came from. That forgetting is the Central Limit Theorem.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
The Central Limit Theorem
In plain language: if you add up or average enough independent random pieces, the result is approximately Normal — no matter what distribution the pieces came from. Francis Galton called it the “supreme law of unreason”: order emerging reliably out of individual chaos. (Grinstead and Snell 1997)
Stated carefully: let X_1, X_2, \dots be independent and identically distributed with finite mean \mu and finite variance \sigma^2. Then as n \to \infty, the standardized sample mean converges in distribution to a standard Normal:

Z_n \;=\; \frac{\bar{X}_n - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \;\xrightarrow{d}\; \mathcal{N}(0, 1).

Two things deserve emphasis, because they are where the theorem is most often misremembered:

What it needs: independence, and a finite mean and variance. It does not need the data to be Normal — that’s the whole point. A version even allows the pieces to have different distributions, as long as no single one dominates the sum. (Ross 2018)
How fast: remarkably fast for symmetric data — a fair die’s average looks bell-shaped by n \approx 10. The famous “n \ge 30” is only a rule of thumb, and a bad one for heavily skewed data, which can need far more. (Bruce et al. 2020; Reinhart 2015)
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Standard deviation vs standard error
This is the second great confusion, and it costs people dearly. (Reinhart 2015)

Standard deviation (\sigma) measures the spread of individual data points. How much do people differ from the average person? That’s the SD.
Standard error (SE) measures the spread of a statistic across hypothetical samples. It is the standard deviation of the sampling distribution. How much would my estimate bounce around if I redid the study? That’s the SE. (Bruce et al. 2020)

They are linked by the single most useful formula in applied statistics:

\text{SE}(\bar{X}) \;=\; \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}.

The \sqrt{n} is doing quiet, brutal work. Because uncertainty shrinks with the square root of the sample size, to halve your standard error you must quadruple your sample. This is why precision is expensive, and why a poll of 1,200 is “good enough” while a poll of 4,800 is only twice as good. (Bruce et al. 2020)
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Worked examples
An astronomer measures a distance. (Ross 2018) Atmospheric distortion and human error make any single telescope reading noisy. So the astronomer takes n independent readings and averages them. By the CLT, that average is approximately Normal around the true distance — and its spread, the SE, shrinks like \sigma/\sqrt{n}. More readings, sharper answer.
A pollster samples a nation. (Grinstead and Snell 1997) The CLT is why a sample of roughly 1,200 people pins down a national preference to about a \pm 3\% margin — and, strikingly, why that barely depends on whether the country has 100,000 or 100 million people. It’s the sample size that drives precision, not the population size.
Female village councils in India. (Gerber and Green 2012) When researchers asked whether reserving council-head seats for women shifted budget priorities, they used the sampling distribution of the estimated effect to ask: is the spending difference we observed bigger than what random assignment alone could have produced? That comparison is the inference.
The RAND Health Insurance Experiment. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) Before trusting any treatment effect, the researchers used standard errors to check that groups assigned to different insurance plans were balanced at baseline — comparable starting points, within the bounds of chance.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong in practice




Thinking your data should be Normal. The CLT is about the sampling distribution of a statistic, not your raw observations. Social-science data is routinely skewed and long-tailed (income, donations, durations). Forcing a Normal model onto raw data underestimates extreme “black swan” values. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Trusting n \ge 30 blindly. For strongly skewed data, t-tests and Normal approximations can be badly off even at moderate n — Reinhart’s example is charitable donations, where a few huge gifts wreck the approximation. (Reinhart 2015)
Eyeballing overlapping error bars. Two confidence intervals can overlap and the difference can still be significant. “Your eyeball is not a well-defined statistical procedure” — run the actual test. (Reinhart 2015)
Treating the bootstrap as a cure for small samples. Resampling your own data reveals the variability already present; it does not manufacture information you never collected. It frees you from assuming Normality, not from needing enough data. (Bruce et al. 2020)
The gambler’s fallacy and optional stopping. A run of heads does not make tails “due” — the Law of Large Numbers governs the long run, not short streaks. And peeking at your data repeatedly and stopping the moment p < 0.05 massively inflates false positives. (Grinstead and Snell 1997; Reinhart 2015)
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Ross, A First Course in Probability — the sample mean as a random variable; the formal CLT; the astronomer example.
Grinstead & Snell, Introduction to Probability — CLT via simulation; the polling example; the Law of Large Numbers and gambler’s fallacy.
Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — data vs sampling distribution; SD vs SE; the bootstrap; the “raw data isn’t Normal” warning.
Reinhart, Statistics Done Wrong — small-sample failures; overlapping error bars; optional-stopping inflation of false positives.
Gerber & Green, Field Experiments — the sampling distribution as “every possible random assignment”; the India village-council study.
Angrist & Pischke, Mostly Harmless Econometrics — standard errors for baseline balance in the RAND experiment.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Appendix · the full mathematics

Mean and variance of the sample mean. Let X_1,\dots,X_n be i.i.d. with \mathbb{E}[X_i]=\mu and \operatorname{Var}(X_i)=\sigma^2. Then by linearity of expectation,

\mathbb{E}[\bar{X}] = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \mathbb{E}[X_i] = \mu,

so the sample mean is unbiased. For the variance, independence makes the cross terms vanish:

\operatorname{Var}(\bar{X})
= \operatorname{Var}\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right)
= \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^n \operatorname{Var}(X_i)
= \frac{1}{n^2}\,(n\sigma^2)
= \frac{\sigma^2}{n}.

Taking the square root gives the standard error, \text{SE}(\bar X)=\sigma/\sqrt{n}, and with it the \sqrt{n} rule.
The Central Limit Theorem (Lindeberg–Lévy form). Define the standardized mean

Z_n = \frac{\bar X_n - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt n} = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt n}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \mu).

Then for every real z,

\lim_{n\to\infty} \Pr(Z_n \le z) = \Phi(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{z} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\,e^{-t^2/2}\,dt .

Sketch of why. Work with the characteristic function \varphi(t)=\mathbb{E}[e^{itZ_n}]. Writing Y_i=(X_i-\mu)/\sigma (mean 0, variance 1) and using independence,

\varphi_{Z_n}(t) = \left[\varphi_Y\!\left(\tfrac{t}{\sqrt n}\right)\right]^{n}.

A second-order Taylor expansion gives \varphi_Y(s) = 1 - \tfrac{1}{2}s^2 + o(s^2), so

\varphi_{Z_n}(t) = \left[1 - \frac{t^2}{2n} + o\!\left(\tfrac{1}{n}\right)\right]^{n}
\;\longrightarrow\; e^{-t^2/2},

which is the characteristic function of the standard Normal. Lévy’s continuity theorem then delivers convergence in distribution. The finite-variance assumption is exactly what makes the quadratic term — and nothing higher — survive in the limit; when variance is infinite (heavy tails), the limit is a different, non-Normal stable law, which is the deep reason the CLT can fail.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Connections

Builds on: random variables, expectation and variance (→ Foundations).
Leads to: confidence intervals and hypothesis testing, where the sampling distribution becomes the engine of inference.
Reappears in: the standard errors attached to every regression coefficient and every causal estimate.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Study with NotebookLM
Take these prompts to the source library to push past the synthesis above:

“Walk me through Grinstead & Snell’s simulation argument for the Central Limit Theorem, step by step, as if I’d never seen it.”
“Give me three more examples from these sources where confusing the standard deviation with the standard error led to a wrong conclusion.”
“Explain the bootstrap as presented in Practical Statistics for Data Scientists, and be precise about what it can and cannot fix.”
“What exactly does Statistics Done Wrong say about optional stopping, and how badly does it inflate the false-positive rate?”
“When does the Central Limit Theorem fail or converge slowly? Find the conditions discussed across these sources.”
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: I · Foundations: Probability & Data
text: The bedrock. Before inference, before regression, before causality, you need a feel for randomness and the objects we use to tame it — and a feel for real data, which is messier than any textbook’s. The aim is not rigour for its own sake but fluency: enough probability and data sense that later ideas feel inevitable rather than arbitrary.

title: I · Foundations: Probability & Data

Where this leads
Into Part II · Inference: once you accept that a statistic has a sampling distribution, confidence intervals and hypothesis tests become careful ways of reading that distribution. The bridge article — Sampling Distributions & the CLT — already lives there.

title: Appendix · Mathematics
text: The house rule of this encyclopedia is that mathematics serves intuition, not the reverse. In the main entries, math appears inline only where it clarifies a principle or a deviation. The full derivations — the proof of thought behind a claim — live here and in the collapsible appendices at the foot of each article, so they are always reachable and never mandatory.

title: Appendix · Mathematics

Worked example, already live
The flagship entry’s appendix derives the variance of the sample mean and sketches the characteristic-function proof of the CLT — a model of the level of detail this section targets. See Sampling distributions & the CLT → Appendix.

title: How to use this
text: A reusable, growing reference for becoming a quantitative social science researcher. It is opinionated about pedagogy: intuition and use-cases lead, mathematics follows only where it clarifies a principle or a deviation, and the full derivations live in appendices. The goal is a reader who can move from undergraduate foundations to the academic rigour of causal research and the realities of field and client work.

title: How to use this

The goal is a reader who can move from undergraduate foundations to the academic rigour of causal research and the realities of field and client work.

title: How to use this
text:
The learning loop
This site is one half of a loop. The other half is NotebookLM, where the full source library lives. The intended cycle:

Read an article here for the structured, intuition-first synthesis.
Interrogate the primary sources in NotebookLM using the Study with NotebookLM prompts at the foot of each article — get audio overviews, ask “explain this with a different example,” or push on the math.
Return and let the article be your map; NotebookLM is the territory.


Why this split? The website is the scaffold for rigorous engagement — a logical sequence and a consistent lens. NotebookLM is the engagement itself — grounded, conversational, source-cited. Neither replaces the other.


The source library
The companion NotebookLM holds 22 canonical sources, including:

Probability
Ross A First Course; Grinstead & Snell; Ross Probability Models


Applied statistics
Practical Statistics for Data Scientists; Statistics Done Wrong; Albers


Econometrics
Wooldridge; Angrist & Pischke Mastering ’Metrics & Mostly Harmless; Panel Data; Moffatt Experimetrics


Causal inference
Cunningham The Mixtape; Gerber & Green Field Experiments


Machine learning
ISLR; ML for Social & Behavioral Research; ML for Experiments


Computing (R)
Dayal ×2; Explanatory Statistical Modeling in R


Methods & critique
Bryman Social Research Methods; Muller The Tyranny of Metrics



Every article cites which of these its claims come from, so you can always trace an idea back to its source and read further.

title: How to use this
section: The house style (for contributors — including future-me)
text:
The house style (for contributors — including future-me)
Articles are written to a fixed template so the encyclopedia stays coherent as it grows:

In one sentence — a single italic intuition kernel under the title.
Why it matters — the motivating use-case before any notation.
Intuition — plain language, concrete examples, analogies that hold up.
Worked examples — taken from the source library, with real studies where possible.
The math, where it earns its place — inline and minimal; if a derivation is longer than the idea it supports, it goes to the appendix.
Common pitfalls — how the concept is misused in practice (the Statistics Done Wrong lens is a recurring guest).
From the sources — explicit per-source attribution.
Appendix · the full mathematics — wrapped in a tinted block the reader may skip.
Connections — links up and down the curriculum.
Study with NotebookLM — 3–5 prompts to take back to the sources.

Math uses common sense first and proof second: notation is introduced because the prose demanded it, never to decorate.

title: How to use this
text:
Building locally
# Render the whole site
quarto render

# Live-preview while editing
quarto preview
Executable code blocks (e.g. simulations) run on a Python virtual environment in .venv/ with numpy and matplotlib. R support can be added per-document later — Quarto handles both engines in the same project.

title: Appendix · Computing
text: The source library is R-first (Dayal’s two volumes, Explanatory Statistical Modeling in R, ISLR’s labs), so this encyclopedia treats R as the default working language for social science. Python appears where it is the natural choice — simulations, machine learning, data wrangling at scale — and Quarto runs both engines in the same project without friction.

title: Appendix · Computing
text:
Already live
The flagship entry includes an executable Python simulation of the Central Limit Theorem (folded by default). It runs on the project’s .venv with numpy and matplotlib, and is the template for how live code appears throughout: short, seeded, and illustrating exactly one idea. See Sampling distributions & the CLT.

title: 0 · Overture: How Research Works
text:
A newcomer who jumps straight into probability learns tools without a job. This overture gives the job first: what quantitative social science is trying to do, and the shape of a study from question to credible conclusion. It is deliberately light on technique and heavy on framing, vocabulary, and judgement — the scaffolding everything technical hangs on.
It plants three seeds the whole manual grows:

title: 0 · Overture: How Research Works
text:
Where this leads
Straight into Part I · Foundations: Probability & Data — the grammar of uncertainty that every later technique is written in.

title: II · Inference
text: Inference is the discipline of saying something about a population you cannot fully see, using a sample you can, while being honest about the uncertainty. Every tool here is a way of reading the sampling distribution — the idea that turns a single number into a claim.

title: II · Inference
text:
A recurring theme
This part carries the manual’s critical conscience: Statistics Done Wrong sits beside every test, because knowing how inference is misused (p-hacking, optional stopping, underpowered studies) is part of knowing how to use it.

title: II · Inference
text:
Where this leads
Inference is the grammar that Part III · Regression speaks: a regression coefficient is an estimate, and everything you learn here about uncertainty attaches to it.

title: III · Statistical Modelling: Regression
text: This is where statistics meets the social world’s questions. The centre of gravity is regression: a tool for holding things constant, summarising relationships, and — with care — estimating effects. The undergraduate core: OLS, its assumptions, its inference, its honest diagnostics, and the limited-dependent-variable models for outcomes that aren’t continuous.

title: III · Statistical Modelling: Regression
text:
The bridge to causality
Mastering ’Metrics frames regression as a way of making “other things equal.” That framing is the hinge between description and Causal Inference: a coefficient is only an effect if you have an argument for why the comparison is fair.

title: III · Statistical Modelling: Regression
text:
Where this leads
Onward to Part IV · Predictive Modelling — the computational turn, where the goal shifts from explaining to predicting, and the same regression machinery is judged by a completely different standard.

title: V · The Summit: Causal Inference
text: The summit of the whole manual, and the centre of gravity of the source library. The question is deceptively simple — what is the effect of X on Y? — and answering it honestly requires a framework for imagining the world that didn’t happen, plus the design and computational tools built in every part before this one.

title: V · The Summit: Causal Inference
text:
The “Furious Five”
The applied core, as Angrist & Pischke frame it: randomised experiments, regression adjustment, instrumental variables, regression discontinuity, and difference-in-differences. Each is a different argument for why a comparison is fair — a different way of approximating the experiment you wish you could have run.

title: V · The Summit: Causal Inference
text:
Where this leads
Down from the summit to Part VI · Doing Research in the World: having learned what a credible causal claim is, you learn what survives contact with measurement, incentives, and a client who needs an answer by Friday.

title: ML Track (separate)
text:
Machine learning is kept deliberately separate from the causal main track, and bridged to it only where it earns a place. The reason is the argument made in Predictive Modelling: ML is, by default, the predictive culture. Pretending its tools answer causal questions is one of the most common and costly confusions in applied work.
That said, the frontier of social science increasingly uses ML in service of causal goals — estimating treatment-effect heterogeneity, handling high-dimensional controls — and this track follows that bridge carefully.

title: ML Track (separate)
text:
The bridge back
Every entry here points back to the main spine: a prediction is not an effect, and a feature importance is not a cause. The ML track sharpens your tools without loosening your causal discipline.
